title: Form 13B — Net Family Property Statement
description: Used to calculate the equalization of net family property under Part I of the Family Law Act. Each party completes their own Form 13B. The difference between the two net family property values determines the equalization payment owed from one spouse to the other.

steps.title: About This Form
steps.description: Form 13B calculates your net family property (NFP) — the foundation of property equalization in Ontario.

fields.label: How equalization works in Ontario
fields.content:
**Ontario's equalization of net family property** means that when a marriage ends, each spouse keeps what they brought in — but they share equally in what was accumulated during the marriage.

**How it works:**
1. Calculate your assets and debts on the **valuation date** (usually the date you separated)
2. Subtract your assets and debts on the **date of marriage** (your starting point)
3. Subtract any **excluded property** (gifts, inheritances, and certain other assets)
4. The result is your **Net Family Property (NFP)**
5. The spouse with the higher NFP pays the other half the difference — this is the **equalization payment**

**You need:** your separation date, your marriage date, values of all property at both dates, and records of any gifts or inheritances.

fields.label: Courthouse
options: St. Catharines — Superior Court of Justice
Thunder Bay — Superior Court of Justice

fields.label: Court file number
Applicant's full legal name
Respondent's full legal name
You are completing this form as

options.label: The Applicant
The Respondent

fields.label: Date of marriage
fields.helpText: The date your marriage was legally performed.

fields.label: Valuation date (usually your separation date)
fields.helpText: The valuation date is the earliest of: the date you separated, the date a divorce order is made, or the date of an event triggering equalization. Usually this is your separation date.

steps.title: Assets on Valuation Date
steps.description: List the value of everything you owned on the valuation date (your separation date). Include all assets even if they were partially or fully inherited or gifted — you will deduct excluded property later.

fields.label: Family home — value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Enter the fair market value. Use a recent appraisal if available. If you do not own or have an interest in the family home, enter 0.

fields.label: Family home — address
fields.placeholder: e.g. 123 Main St, Toronto ON M5V 1A1

fields.label: Other real estate — total value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Include rental properties, cottages, vacant land, or any other real property you have an interest in.

fields.label: Describe other real estate
fields.placeholder: e.g. Rental property at 456 Oak Ave, Toronto — value $480,000 / Cottage at 789 Lake Rd, Muskoka — value $320,000

fields.label: Bank accounts, savings, GICs — total balance on valuation date
fields.helpText: Total balance across all accounts — chequing, savings, GICs, high-interest savings, TFSAs. Include accounts in your name only and any joint accounts.

fields.label: Describe bank accounts
fields.placeholder: e.g. TD chequing — $4,200 / RBC savings — $12,500 / TFSA at TD — $38,000

fields.label: RRSPs, RRIFs, LIRAs — total value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Use the market value before tax. Note: the tax owing on withdrawal is often deducted separately as a liability or negotiated between parties.

fields.label: Pension plans — value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Use the commuted value (transfer value) of your pension. For defined benefit pensions, you may need an actuarial valuation. Contact your pension administrator.

fields.label: Describe pension plan(s)
fields.placeholder: e.g. OMERS defined benefit pension — commuted value on valuation date: $185,000 / Company RRSP/DC pension — $52,000

fields.label: Non-registered investments — total value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Include stocks, bonds, mutual funds, ETFs, investment accounts held outside an RRSP or TFSA.

fields.label: Business interests — value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Include shares or ownership interest in any business. A business valuation by a qualified valuator may be required. Enter the fair market value of your interest.

fields.label: Describe business interests
fields.placeholder: e.g. 50% ownership in ABC Plumbing Inc. — valued at $240,000 by business valuator John Smith, CA·CBV, on [date]

fields.label: Vehicles — total value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Include cars, trucks, motorcycles, boats, RVs, snowmobiles, etc. Use Canadian Black Book or similar for market value.

fields.label: Life insurance — cash surrender value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Only include policies with a cash surrender value (whole life, universal life). Term life insurance has no cash value — enter 0.

fields.label: Household contents and personal property — value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Include furniture, appliances, electronics, jewelry, art, collections, and other personal property. Use fair market value (what you could sell it for today).

fields.label: Money owed to you (loans receivable, legal settlements) — value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Include any money others owe you — loans you made, pending legal settlements, expected tax refunds, etc.

fields.label: Other assets — value on valuation date
Describe other assets
fields.placeholder: e.g. Cryptocurrency — $8,500 / Structured settlement — $25,000 / Intellectual property rights — $12,000

fields.label: TOTAL assets on valuation date
fields.helpText: Add up all assets listed above.

steps.title: Debts on Valuation Date
steps.description: List all debts and liabilities you owed on the valuation date. These reduce your net family property.

fields.label: Mortgage on family home — balance owing on valuation date
Mortgages on other properties — total balance owing on valuation date
Vehicle loans — total balance owing on valuation date
Credit card balances — total owing on valuation date
Lines of credit — total balance owing on valuation date
Student loans — balance owing on valuation date
Personal loans (family, bank) — balance owing on valuation date
Business debts you are personally responsible for — balance owing on valuation date
Income taxes owing (including estimated deferred taxes on RRSPs) — on valuation date
fields.helpText: Include any income tax owing to CRA, and optionally an estimate of the tax you would owe if you cashed out your RRSP/pension (parties often negotiate whether to include this).

fields.label: Other debts — total owing on valuation date
Describe other debts
fields.placeholder: e.g. Money owed to parents for home down payment loan — $20,000 / Unpaid legal fees — $8,000

fields.label: TOTAL debts on valuation date
fields.helpText: Add up all debts listed above.

fields.label: Net value on valuation date (assets minus debts)
fields.helpText: Subtract total debts from total assets.

steps.title: Assets on Date of Marriage
steps.description: List what you owned on the day you were married. This is subtracted from your valuation date net to calculate growth during the marriage.

fields.label: Date of marriage assets
fields.content:
List the value of your assets on the date of your marriage. These represent what you brought into the marriage — they are subtracted so that property you had before the marriage is not shared.

**Important:** The family home you lived in at separation is NOT deducted even if you owned it before marriage — it is always included in the equalization calculation.

fields.label: Bank accounts and savings — balance on date of marriage
RRSPs, investments — value on date of marriage
Real estate (excluding the matrimonial home) — value on date of marriage
Business interests — value on date of marriage
Pension — value on date of marriage
Vehicles — value on date of marriage
Other assets on date of marriage
Describe other assets on date of marriage
fields.placeholder: e.g. Inheritance received before marriage — $30,000 / Personal property — $5,000

fields.label: TOTAL assets on date of marriage

steps.title: Debts on Date of Marriage
steps.description: List the debts you owed on the day you were married. These reduce your date-of-marriage deduction.

fields.label: Mortgage(s) — balance owing on date of marriage
Vehicle loans — balance owing on date of marriage
Student loans — balance owing on date of marriage
Credit card and line of credit balances — on date of marriage
Other debts on date of marriage
TOTAL debts on date of marriage
Net value on date of marriage (assets minus debts)
fields.helpText: This is the amount subtracted from your valuation date net to account for what you brought into the marriage.

steps.title: Excluded Property
steps.description: Certain property is excluded from equalization even if you owned it on the valuation date. List any property that qualifies as excluded.

fields.label: What property is excluded?
fields.content:
Under the Family Law Act, the following property received **during the marriage** is excluded from equalization:

- **Gifts or inheritances** received from a third party during the marriage (not from your spouse)
- **Proceeds from a life insurance policy** received during the marriage
- **Damages or settlement** for personal injury, nervous shock, or a prescribed right
- **Property traceable to** any of the above (e.g. if you invested an inheritance and it grew, the original inheritance value is excluded)

**Important:** The increase in value of excluded property is NOT excluded — only the original value of the excluded property itself.

fields.label: Do you have any excluded property?

options.label: Yes
No

fields.label: List each excluded property item
Description of the excluded property
fields.placeholder: e.g. Inheritance from my father's estate / Gift of money from my parents / Personal injury settlement

fields.label: Type of exclusion

options.label: Gift from a third party (not from spouse)
Inheritance
Life insurance proceeds
Personal injury damages or settlement
Property traceable to an excluded source

fields.label: Date received
fields.helpText: Approximate date you received this property during the marriage.

fields.label: Value of the excluded property (at the time you received it, or at valuation date if it still exists in original form)
Does this property still exist in its original form on the valuation date?

options.label: Yes — still exists (e.g. still in a bank account or investment)
Partly — some was spent or converted, some remains
No — it was spent or no longer traceable

fields.label: Notes on how you can trace this exclusion
fields.placeholder: e.g. The $45,000 inheritance I received in 2019 was deposited into my TD savings account. I have bank records showing the deposit and the account balance over time. I kept the inheritance separate from joint funds.

fields.label: TOTAL excluded property value
fields.helpText: Add up the values of all excluded property items listed above.

steps.title: Net Family Property Calculation
steps.description: Calculate your net family property. This is the number that determines whether you owe an equalization payment or are entitled to receive one.

fields.label: The formula
fields.content:
**Net Family Property (NFP) = A − B − C**

Where:
- **A** = Net value on valuation date (total assets minus total debts at separation)
- **B** = Net value on date of marriage (total assets minus total debts at marriage)
- **C** = Excluded property

If the result is negative, your NFP is treated as zero.

The spouse with the **higher NFP** pays the other spouse **half the difference**. That payment is the equalization payment.

fields.label: Line A — Net value on valuation date
fields.helpText: Copy from Step 3: Net value on valuation date.

fields.label: Line B — Net value on date of marriage
fields.helpText: Copy from Step 5: Net value on date of marriage.

fields.label: Line C — Total excluded property
fields.helpText: Copy from Step 6: Total excluded property value. Enter 0 if no excluded property.

fields.label: Your Net Family Property (A minus B minus C)
fields.helpText: If this number is negative, your NFP is zero for equalization purposes.

fields.label: Is your calculated NFP negative?

options.label: Yes — my NFP is negative, so I will report it as zero
No — my NFP is zero or positive

fields.label: Your final Net Family Property (zero if negative)
fields.helpText: If your calculated NFP above was negative, enter 0 here. Otherwise enter the same number as above.

steps.title: Equalization Claim
steps.description: Based on your NFP, describe what equalization payment you are claiming or expect to owe.

fields.label: What is your estimate of the other party's Net Family Property?
fields.helpText: If you know or can estimate the other party's NFP, enter it here. The equalization payment is half the difference between the two NFPs.

fields.label: Estimated equalization payment
fields.helpText: If your NFP is lower than the other party's, you are owed an equalization payment = (Their NFP − Your NFP) ÷ 2.

fields.label: Based on your estimate

options.label: I am owed an equalization payment from the other party
I owe an equalization payment to the other party
The NFPs appear roughly equal — no significant payment either way
I don't have enough information to estimate

fields.label: Are there any specific assets or valuations that are in dispute with the other party?
fields.placeholder: e.g. The parties disagree on the value of the family home — I believe it is worth $780,000 based on a recent appraisal; the other party claims $850,000. The parties also disagree on the valuation of the other party's dental practice.
fields.helpText: Identifying disputed valuations helps the court understand what needs to be resolved.

fields.label: Are you asking the court to award an amount other than the standard equalization payment?
fields.helpText: In rare cases, a court can order an unequal division of property if the standard equalization payment would be unconscionable (shockingly unfair).

options.label: Yes — I am asking for an unequal division
No — I am asking for the standard equalization payment

fields.label: Explain why an unequal division is warranted
fields.placeholder: e.g. The other party deliberately dissipated significant family assets in the two years before separation by making large unauthorized withdrawals from joint accounts and gambling losses totalling approximately $85,000. Standard equalization would be unconscionable in these circumstances.

steps.title: Declaration
steps.description: Confirm the accuracy of your Net Family Property Statement.

fields.label: What documents do you have to support the values in this statement?

options.label: Mortgage statement as of valuation date
Bank and investment statements as of valuation date
RRSP/RRIF statements as of valuation date
Pension commuted value letter from administrator
Property appraisal or real estate valuation
Business valuation report
Vehicle valuation (Canadian Black Book or appraisal)
Income tax returns and Notices of Assessment
Bank/investment statements from date of marriage
Estate documents or gift letters (for excluded property)

fields.label: Declaration
fields.text: I declare that the information in this Net Family Property Statement is true and complete to the best of my knowledge and belief. I understand that providing false information to the court is a serious matter.
fields.checkboxLabel: I confirm this declaration is true and accurate.

fields.label: Date

pdfMapping.notes: Form 13B — Net Family Property Statement. Each party completes separately. Used to calculate equalization payment under the Family Law Act. See ontario.ca/page/family-law-forms for the current version.
